request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"ubiome::biotech","name":"ubiome","one_liner":"US microbiome sequencing company that sold mail-in gut-testing kits with an app on a recurring subscription, shut down after an FBI raid and bankruptcy over Medicare insurance fraud in 2019.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":4,"rationale":"Both use a consumer-facing, recurring subscription model bundled with a digital health app. However, uBiome's core product was a physical mail-in lab-testing kit (one-time test event, billed repeatedly and often without consent), whereas the new pitch is a pure software subscription combining symptom tracking with on-demand telehealth — no physical product involved."},"market":{"score":4,"rationale":"Both target health-conscious consumers and overlap on the 'quantified self / personal health data' theme. uBiome focused on US consumers curious about microbiome data (not specifically chronic-illness patients), while the new pitch targets diagnosed chronic-illness patients in Germany and France — a different geography, a more defined patient population, and a regulated EU market."},"gtm":{"score":3,"rationale":"uBiome used crowdfunding, Y Combinator, and direct-to-consumer marketing to reach early adopters in the US. The new pitch targets EU patients, likely requiring partnerships with patient advocacy groups, GPs, or statutory health insurers (Krankenkassen / Sécurité Sociale) — a fundamentally different GTM motion with regulatory reimbursement considerations."},"stage_scale":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both are early-stage consumer healthtech startups with subscription revenue and a digital app component. uBiome raised seed through Series B from notable investors before collapse; the new pitch appears pre-launch or very early. Stage is loosely comparable but geography and regulatory environment differ significantly."}},"why_similar":"Both are direct-to-consumer health subscription businesses that bundle a data-collection product with an app and use recurring monthly billing. Both target individual consumers (not clinicians as primary buyers) and sit at the intersection of health data, digital apps, and ongoing subscription monetization. uBiome's subscription-plus-app structure, consumer focus, and healthtech sector make it a meaningful, if imperfect, analog.","where_diverged":"At least four concrete dimensions separate the new pitch from uBiome: (1) Geography — the new pitch targets Germany and France (EU, GDPR, MDR, national health insurance systems) vs. uBiome's US market with FDA and Medicare rules; (2) Product type — pure SaaS (symptom tracking + telehealth) vs. a physical mail-in laboratory testing kit; (3) Target condition — chronic-illness patients with ongoing management needs vs. wellness-curious consumers exploring microbiome novelty; (4) Revenue mechanism — straightforward monthly software subscription vs. uBiome's hybrid model that also billed private insurers and Medicare, creating the fraudulent-billing exposure that destroyed the company.","failure_causes":["Medicare/insurance fraud — repeated billing without patient consent","FBI investigation and office raid triggering operational collapse","Founder misconduct and investor fraud ($60M SEC charges)","Ethical failures in informed consent for citizen-science data collection","Regulatory and IRB compliance shortcuts at launch","Aggressive growth culture overriding compliance guardrails","Chapter 7 liquidation after failed Chapter 11 reorganization"],"lessons_for_input":["Never build a revenue model that depends on third-party payer reimbursement (insurers, public health funds) without airtight compliance infrastructure in place from day one — uBiome's downfall was unbounded billing against Medicare.","In EU chronic-illness healthtech, engage with national reimbursement bodies (Krankenkassen, CNAM) and obtain digital health certifications (DiGA in Germany, CE-MDR if applicable) before scaling — regulatory legitimacy is a moat, not a checkbox.","Separate clinical claims from wellness claims rigorously; uBiome blurred these lines and faced bioethics backlash and regulatory scrutiny long before the fraud surfaced.","Build transparent, auditable billing and data-consent flows from the start; recurring subscription models in healthcare are under intense regulatory scrutiny in both the US and EU.","Governance and founder accountability matter as much as product — uBiome's co-founders falsified credentials and ultimately became fugitives; ensure board-level oversight and independent compliance functions early."]}
